chunk_summary: This section discusses waist circumference reference charts and waist-to-height ratio thresholds for assessing central adiposity and cardiometabolic risk in children, emphasizing standardized methods and India-specific guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of waist circumference in assessing central adiposity and cardiometabolic risk.
Learn the importance of using standardized reference charts for waist circumference measurements.
Explore the utility of waist-to-height ratio as a screening tool for central adiposity.
Recognize the significance of tracking longitudinal changes in waist circumference for obesity management.
key_facts: Waist circumference provides context to BMI but requires standardized reference charts for consistent interpretation.
India-specific waist circumference percentiles, such as the Khadilkar reference (2–18 years), are preferred for clinical use.
A waist percentile around the 70th percentile can flag higher cardiometabolic risk in Indian children.
Waist-to-height ratio (WHtR) above 0.5 is a widely used threshold for screening central adiposity, especially in adolescents.
Standardized measurement technique: midpoint between lowest rib and iliac crest, tape horizontal, end-expiration.
reference_quote: The Khadilkar multicenter reference (2–18 years) is widely cited and supports a pragmatic approach used in Indian guidance: a waist percentile around the 70th percentile can flag higher cardiometabolic risk.
document_key_topics: Waist circumference reference charts for Indian children
